title: Frequently asked questions
metaTitle: IMG to Doctor in Australia: FAQ
metaDescription: Plain answers for IMGs on AHPRA registration, AMC exams, English tests, visas, the moratorium and timelines. Linked to official sources.
summaryMarkdown: Common questions from international medical graduates (IMGs) about becoming a doctor in Australia, which pathway fits you, exams and English tests, qualification verification, visas, the Medicare moratorium, and how long it all takes. Each answer points to an official source so you can check the detail and confirm your own situation.

sections.heading: How to use these answers
sections.bodyMarkdown: These answers are general information only, not personal advice about your registration, immigration or employment. Every answer links to an official source so you can read the full detail, and the rules can change, always confirm your situation directly with AHPRA, the Medical Board of Australia and the AMC before you act.

faq.question: Which registration pathway is right for me?
faq.answerMarkdown: There are four pathways: **Standard** (most IMGs, requiring AMC exams), **Competent Authority** (for doctors trained or examined in the UK, Ireland, USA, Canada or New Zealand, who skip the AMC exams), **Specialist** (for doctors with a recognised specialist qualification) and the faster **Expedited Specialist** route. Which fits depends on where you trained and whether you hold a specialist qualification. Try the [pathway finder](/finder), or read [Standard](/pathways/standard), [Competent Authority](/pathways/competent-authority) and [Specialist](/pathways/specialist).

faq.question: Do I need a job offer before I start?
faq.answerMarkdown: For most pathways you need an approved supervised-practice position before you can hold the registration that lets you work, because provisional and limited registration are tied to a specific job and supervisor. A job offer also usually supports your work visa, so registration, employment and visa tend to move together. See [Standard pathway](/pathways/standard) and [visas](/visas).

faq.question: Do I have to sit the AMC exams?
faq.answerMarkdown: Not always. On the Standard pathway you pass the AMC CAT MCQ exam then the AMC clinical exam (or a workplace-based assessment). On the Competent Authority and Specialist pathways you generally don't sit the AMC exams, because your training or specialist qualification is assessed instead. See [Standard pathway](/pathways/standard) and [Competent Authority](/pathways/competent-authority).

faq.question: What English requirements do I need to meet?
faq.answerMarkdown: You must meet the Medical Board's English language skills standard, which can be met through your education or an approved test (OET, IELTS Academic, PTE Academic, TOEFL iBT or Cambridge C1/C2). From 23 April 2026 the minimum test scores were updated, so check the current accepted-tests page before booking. See [English requirements](/english).

faq.question: How do I get my qualifications verified?
faq.answerMarkdown: Your primary medical qualification must be verified at the source before registration, through the AMC's primary source verification process, your issuing institution confirms your documents directly via the ECFMG EPIC / MyIntealth system rather than you submitting copies. Allow time, as it depends on your medical school responding. See [Standard pathway](/pathways/standard).

faq.question: Do I need a specialist college?
faq.answerMarkdown: If you want recognition as a specialist (e.g. GP, physician, anaesthetist, psychiatrist), the relevant Australian college assesses your qualifications and advises the Medical Board. If you're not seeking specialist recognition, you don't need a college and would use the Standard or Competent Authority pathway. See [colleges](/colleges) and [Specialist pathway](/pathways/specialist).

faq.question: What is the expedited specialist pathway?
faq.answerMarkdown: A faster route to specialist registration for certain overseas-trained specialists from comparable systems, where the Board grants registration with conditions (such as supervised practice and orientation) instead of a full college comparability assessment. It covers a growing list of specialties, general medicine and general paediatrics were added from 19 January 2026, alongside general practice, anaesthesia, psychiatry and obstetrics and gynaecology. See [Expedited Specialist](/pathways/expedited-specialist).

faq.question: What's the difference between registration and a visa?
faq.answerMarkdown: Registration is permission from the Medical Board (through AHPRA) to practise medicine; a visa is permission from the Department of Home Affairs to live and work in Australia. They're separate approvals from separate bodies, and you generally need both. See [visas](/visas) and [Medicare](/medicare).

faq.question: Can I work anywhere in Australia, or does the moratorium apply?
faq.answerMarkdown: Many IMGs are subject to Section 19AB (the 10-year moratorium), which limits where you can access Medicare for up to 10 years from first registration. In practice this usually means working in a Distribution Priority Area (GPs) or a District of Workforce Shortage / area of need (non-GP specialists), unless an exemption applies. See [Medicare](/medicare) and [where you can work](/where-you-can-work).

faq.question: How do I find a location that qualifies under the moratorium?
faq.answerMarkdown: The Department of Health's Health Workforce Locator lets you enter an address and see its classifications, including whether it's a Distribution Priority Area and its Modified Monash Model remoteness rating. Classifications can change, so check the location before you accept a role. See [where you can work](/where-you-can-work).

faq.question: Can my family come with me?
faq.answerMarkdown: Yes. Common doctor work visas such as the Skills in Demand (subclass 482) allow you to include family members, and partners and dependent children can generally live, work and study in Australia for the life of your visa. If you move to permanent residence (e.g. subclass 186), included family members are normally granted it at the same time. See [visas](/visas).

faq.question: How long does it take and how much does it cost?
faq.answerMarkdown: Timelines vary widely, from several months on a Competent Authority or expedited route to a couple of years or more on the Standard pathway, because exams, verification, supervised practice, visas and college assessments each take time and often overlap. Costs add up across AMC fees, English tests, registration and visa charges. See [costs & timelines](/costs-and-timelines).

faq.question: Can I use a migration agent, and do I have to?
faq.answerMarkdown: You can lodge your own visa application, but if you use a paid migration agent in Australia they must be registered with MARA, which you can verify on the official register. Registration with the Medical Board is handled through AHPRA and the AMC and is separate from any migration advice. See [visas](/visas).
